The surgeon begins the procedure by addressing omental adhesions attached to the liver edge and anterior abdominal wall, carefully dissecting these adhesions to expose the gallbladder.

The surgeon continues to expose the gallbladder, which becomes visible amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to remove the omentum from the gallbladder area, preparing for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

When you see this you can only hope that the adhesions aren't going to be this bad down near the base. It almost always isn't. It's almost always just like this at the top. And in this case it was probably from the percutaneous strain that was placed.
The surgeon successfully retracts the omentum, revealing the underlying colon. A grasper is inserted to elevate the gallbladder, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully detaches the colon from the gallbladder, inadvertently entering the liver bed during the process.
The surgeon visually inspects the colon to ensure it is unharmed, expressing satisfaction with its appearance.
The surgeon removes the last epiploic appendage, revealing the base of the gallbladder, indicating readiness to proceed with the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects additional adhesions where the gallbladder adheres to the liver, alternating between the top and bottom of the gallbladder to enable higher retraction.
The surgeon decompresses the gallbladder, revealing the outline of large stones within its lumen, facilitating easier handling and manipulation.
The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder, revealing its base more clearly. Dissection begins on the gallbladder, initially moving laterally before proceeding medially.
The surgeon begins dissection medially on the gallbladder, navigating the surgical field with visible indecision, moving across various areas to achieve optimal positioning.
The surgeon frees the peritoneum from the liver bed, aiding in retracting the gallbladder further off the liver bed to achieve the critical view.
The surgeon uses ICG mode to visualize the gallbladder, revealing a large stone at its bottom. The common bile duct is visible to the right of the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes green bile at the bottom of the gallbladder, indicating the likely location of the cystic duct beneath. The surgeon attempts to clear the area around the cystic duct.
The surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection around the gallbladder, encountering bleeding during the process. Various techniques are employed to control the bleeding, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to clear fat tissue, revealing the cystic duct and artery near the hook instrument, aiming to visualize the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon identifies a posterior artery and uses a Maryland dissector to coagulate it, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.

I'm thinking that's the artery But I really just didn't like what I was doing down there for no reason other than my own nerves So I decided to do a top-down approach a little bit And you'll see how this didn't work out for me in a little bit I

The surgeon encounters bleeding and a small hole in the gallbladder, obstructing visibility. A sponge is used for tamponade to control the bleeding while pushing up on the liver to achieve hemostasis and continue the dissection.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to continue the dissection, addressing bleeding by pinching and cauterizing the area. Surgicil is applied to the bleeding site, followed by a sponge for pressure. The gallbladder is used to lift and hold the sponge in place, while another sponge is introduced to maintain clear visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses an instrument as a retractor to expose the area, attempting to identify the cystic duct. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence is utilized to enhance visualization, although the critical view of safety is not yet fully achieved.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, revealing the critical view as a stone moves out of the way. Confident in identifying the cystic duct, the surgeon proceeds to take the duct, noting its interference with retracting the gallbladder.
The surgeon begins to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully maneuvering around the tissue to locate the artery, which is presumed to be embedded within the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon struggles to manipulate the gallbladder due to the presence of large stones within it, but the gallbladder is gradually being mobilized.

The surgeon stays in an intramural plane, avoiding the liver bed, and identifies the artery, cleaning off surrounding fat. Using ICG mode, the artery is observed pulsating before being taken. The surgeon then works back up the gallbladder, reaching a previously made hole from the top-down approach.
The surgeon places the excised gallbladder into a retrieval bag for removal. The pressure from the sponge and Surgicel has effectively controlled the liver bleeding.
